SFWA Grand Master, Science Fiction and Fantasy Hall of Fame

(born **Alice Mary Norton**, February 17, 1912 – March 17, 2005) was an American writer of [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") and [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy"), who also wrote works of [historical](/wiki/Historical_fiction "Historical fiction") and contemporary fiction. She wrote primarily under the [pen name](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name") **Andre Norton**, but also under **Andrew North** and **Allen Weston**. She was the first woman to be [Gandalf Grand Master](/wiki/Gandalf_Award "Gandalf Award") of Fantasy,[[2]](#cite_note-2) to be [SFWA Grand Master](/wiki/SFWA_Grand_Master "SFWA Grand Master"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and to be inducted by the [Science Fiction and Fantasy Hall of Fame](/wiki/EMP_Museum#Science_Fiction_Hall_of_Fame "EMP Museum").[[4]](#cite_note-SFAwards-4)[[5]](#cite_note-SFWA-5)[[6]](#cite_note-sfhof-old-6)

American science fiction and fantasy writer (1912–2005)

"Andrew North" redirects here. For the journalist, see [Andrew North (journalist)](/wiki/Andrew_North_(journalist) "Andrew North (journalist)").

**Andre Alice Norton** (born **Alice Mary Norton**, February 17, 1912 – March 17, 2005) was an American writer of [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") and [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy"), who also wrote works of [historical](/wiki/Historical_fiction "Historical fiction") and contemporary fiction. She wrote primarily under the [pen name](/wiki/Pen_name "Pen name") **Andre Norton**, but also under **Andrew North** and **Allen Weston**. She was the first woman to be [Gandalf Grand Master](/wiki/Gandalf_Award "Gandalf Award") of Fantasy,[[2]](#cite_note-2) to be [SFWA Grand Master](/wiki/SFWA_Grand_Master "SFWA Grand Master"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and to be inducted by the [Science Fiction and Fantasy Hall of Fame](/wiki/EMP_Museum#Science_Fiction_Hall_of_Fame "EMP Museum").[[4]](#cite_note-SFAwards-4)[[5]](#cite_note-SFWA-5)[[6]](#cite_note-sfhof-old-6)

## Biography and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Andre_Norton&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography and career")]

### Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Andre_Norton&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Biography")]

Alice Mary Norton was born in [Cleveland, Ohio](/wiki/Cleveland,_Ohio "Cleveland, Ohio") in 1912.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Her parents were Adalbert Freely Norton, who owned a rug company, and Bertha Stemm Norton. Alice began writing at [Collinwood High School](/wiki/Collinwood_High_School_(Cleveland) "Collinwood High School (Cleveland)") in Cleveland, under the tutelage of Sylvia Cochrane. She was the editor of a literary page in the school's paper, *The Collinwood Spotlight*, for which she wrote short stories. During this time, she wrote her first book, *Ralestone Luck*, which was eventually published as her second novel in 1938.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

After graduating from high school in 1930, Norton planned to become a teacher, and began studying at [Flora Stone Mather College](/wiki/Flora_Stone_Mather_College "Flora Stone Mather College") of [Western Reserve University](/wiki/Case_Western_Reserve_University "Case Western Reserve University"). However, in 1932 she had to leave because of the Depression and began working for the [Cleveland Library System](/wiki/Cleveland_Public_Library "Cleveland Public Library"),[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) where she remained for 18 years, latterly in the children's section of the Nottingham Branch Library in Cleveland. In a 1996 interview she recalled defending acquisition of *[The Hobbit](/wiki/The_Hobbit "The Hobbit")* by [J. R. R. Tolkien](/wiki/J._R._R._Tolkien "J. R. R. Tolkien") for the library.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1934, she legally changed her name to Andre Alice Norton, a pen name she had adopted for her first book, published later that year, to increase her marketability, since boys were the main audience for fantasy.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

During 1940–1941, she worked as a special librarian in the cataloging department of the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress").[[10]](#cite_note-10) She was involved in a project related to alien citizenship which was abruptly terminated upon the American entry into [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). In 1941 she bought a bookstore called Mystery House in [Mount Rainier, Maryland](/wiki/Mount_Rainier,_Maryland "Mount Rainier, Maryland"), the eastern neighbor of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") The business failed, and she returned to the Cleveland Public Library until 1950, when she retired due to ill health.[[11]](#cite_note-11) She then began working as a [reader](/wiki/Publisher%27s_reader "Publisher's reader") for publisher-editor [Martin Greenberg](/wiki/Martin_Greenberg_(publisher) "Martin Greenberg (publisher)")[[a]](#cite_note-12) at [Gnome Press](/wiki/Gnome_Press "Gnome Press"), a [small press](/wiki/Small_press "Small press") in New York City that focused on science fiction. She remained until 1958, when, with 21 novels published,[[12]](#cite_note-Lehmannhaupt-13)[[13]](#cite_note-kirkus-search-14) she became a full-time professional writer.

As Norton's health became uncertain, she moved to [Winter Park, Florida](/wiki/Winter_Park,_Florida "Winter Park, Florida") in November 1966, where she remained until 1997.[[14]](#cite_note-15) She moved to [Murfreesboro, Tennessee](/wiki/Murfreesboro,_Tennessee "Murfreesboro, Tennessee") in 1997 and was under [hospice care](/wiki/Hospice_care "Hospice care") from February 21, 2005. She died at home on March 17, 2005, of congestive heart failure.

### Literary career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Andre_Norton&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Literary career")]

For a full list of Norton's books, see [Andre Norton bibliography](/wiki/Andre_Norton_bibliography "Andre Norton bibliography").

In 1934, her first book, *The Prince Commands, being sundry adventures of Michael Karl, sometime crown prince & pretender to the throne of Morvania*, with illustrations by [Kate Seredy](/wiki/Kate_Seredy "Kate Seredy"), was published by [D. Appleton–Century Company](/wiki/D._Appleton_%26_Company "D. Appleton & Company") (cataloged by the U.S. Library of Congress as by "André Norton").[[15]](#cite_note-LCC-16)[[16]](#cite_note-LCC1-17) She went on to write several historical novels for the juvenile (now called "young adult") market.

Norton's first published science fiction was a [short story](/wiki/Short_story "Short story"), "The People of the Crater", which appeared under the name "Andrew North" as pages 4–18 of the inaugural 1947 number of *[Fantasy Book](/wiki/Fantasy_Book "Fantasy Book")*, a magazine from [Fantasy Publishing Company, Inc.](/wiki/Fantasy_Publishing_Company,_Inc. "Fantasy Publishing Company, Inc.")[[17]](#cite_note-isfdb-18) Her first fantasy novel, *Huon of the Horn*, published by [Harcourt Brace](/wiki/Harcourt_Brace "Harcourt Brace") under her own name in 1951, adapted the 13th-century story of [Huon, Duke of Bordeaux](/wiki/Huon_of_Bordeaux "Huon of Bordeaux").[[18]](#cite_note-isfdb-huon-19) Her first science fiction novel, *Star Man's Son, 2250 A.D.*, appeared from Harcourt in 1952.[[19]](#cite_note-Locus-20) She became a prolific novelist in the 1950s, with many of her books published for the juvenile market, at least in their original hardcover editions.

As of 1958, when she became a full-time professional writer, *Kirkus* had reviewed 16 of her novels,[[b]](#cite_note-starred-21) and awarded four of them starred reviews.[[13]](#cite_note-kirkus-search-14) Her four starred reviews to 1957 had been awarded for three historical adventure novels—*Follow the Drum* (1942), *Scarface* (1948), *Yankee Privateer* (1955)—and one cold war adventure, *At Swords' Points* (1954). She received four starred reviews subsequently, latest in 1966, including three for science fiction.[[13]](#cite_note-kirkus-search-14)

Norton was twice nominated for the [Hugo Award](/wiki/Hugo_Award "Hugo Award"), in 1964 for the novel *[Witch World](/wiki/Witch_World_(novel) "Witch World (novel)")* and in 1967 for the novelette "Wizard's World". She was nominated three times for the [World Fantasy Award](/wiki/World_Fantasy_Award "World Fantasy Award") for lifetime achievement, winning the award in 1998. Norton won a number of other genre awards and regularly had works appear in the *Locus* annual "best of year" polls.[[4]](#cite_note-SFAwards-4)

She was a founding member of the [Swordsmen and Sorcerers' Guild of America](/wiki/Swordsmen_and_Sorcerers%27_Guild_of_America "Swordsmen and Sorcerers' Guild of America") (SAGA), a loose-knit group of [heroic fantasy](/wiki/Heroic_fantasy "Heroic fantasy") authors founded in the 1960s, led by [Lin Carter](/wiki/Lin_Carter "Lin Carter"), with entry by fantasy credentials alone. Norton was the only woman among the original eight members. Some works by SAGA members were published in [Lin Carter](/wiki/Lin_Carter "Lin Carter")'s *[Flashing Swords!](/wiki/Flashing_Swords! "Flashing Swords!")* anthologies.

In 1976, [Gary Gygax](/wiki/Gary_Gygax "Gary Gygax") invited Norton to play *[Dungeons & Dragons](/wiki/Dungeons_%26_Dragons "Dungeons & Dragons")* in his [Greyhawk](/wiki/Greyhawk "Greyhawk") world. Norton subsequently wrote *[Quag Keep](/wiki/Quag_Keep "Quag Keep")*, which involved a group of characters who travel from the real world to Greyhawk. It was the first novel to be set, at least partially, in the Greyhawk setting and, according to *Alternative Worlds*, the first to be based on *D&D*.[[20]](#cite_note-22) *Quag Keep* was excerpted in Issue 12 of *The Dragon* (February 1978) just prior to the book's release.[[21]](#cite_note-23) She and [Jean Rabe](/wiki/Jean_Rabe "Jean Rabe") were collaborating on the sequel to *Quag Keep* when Norton died. *[Return to Quag Keep](/wiki/Return_to_Quag_Keep "Return to Quag Keep")* was completed by Rabe and published by [Tor Books](/wiki/Tor_Books "Tor Books") in January 2006.[[17]](#cite_note-isfdb-18)

Her final complete novel, *Three Hands for Scorpio*, was published on April 1, 2005. Besides *Return to Quag Keep*, Tor has published two more novels with Norton and Rabe credited as co-authors, *Dragon Mage* (November 2006) and *Taste of Magic* (January 2008).[[17]](#cite_note-isfdb-18)

### Series

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Andre_Norton&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Series")]

Norton wrote more than a dozen [speculative fiction](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction") series, but her longest, and longest-running project was "[Witch World](/wiki/Witch_World "Witch World")", which began with the novel *Witch World* in 1963. The first six novels were [Ace Books](/wiki/Ace_Books "Ace Books") [paperback originals](/wiki/Paperback_originals "Paperback originals") published from 1963 to 1968.[[17]](#cite_note-isfdb-18) From the 1970s most of the books in the series were first published in hardcover editions.[[17]](#cite_note-isfdb-18) From the 1980s some were written by Norton and a co-author, and others were anthologies of short fiction for which she was editor. (Witch World became a [shared universe](/wiki/Shared_universe "Shared universe").)[[c]](#cite_note-mcconchie-24) There were dozens of books in all.[[19]](#cite_note-Locus-20)

The five novels of The Cycle of Oak, Yew, Ash, and Rowan, *To the King a Daughter*, *Knight or Knave*, *A Crown Disowned*, *Dragon Blade*, and *The Knight of the Red Beard*, were written with [Sasha Miller](/wiki/Sasha_Miller "Sasha Miller").[[22]](#cite_note-25) The fifth and last novel was dedicated "To my late collaborator, Andre Norton, whose vision inspired the NordornLand cycle."[[23]](#cite_note-26) ("NordornLand cycle" is another name for this cycle.)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Andre_Norton&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Often called the Grande Dame of Science Fiction and Fantasy by biographers such as J. M. Cornwell,[[24]](#cite_note-27) and organizations such as [Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America](/wiki/Science_Fiction_and_Fantasy_Writers_of_America "Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America"),[[25]](#cite_note-SFWA-norton-28) *[Publishers Weekly](/wiki/Publishers_Weekly "Publishers Weekly")*,[[26]](#cite_note-29) and *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")*, Andre Norton wrote novels for more than 70 years. She had a profound influence on the entire genre, having more than 300 published titles read by at least four generations of science fiction and fantasy readers and writers. Notable authors who cite her influence include [Greg Bear](/wiki/Greg_Bear "Greg Bear"), [Lois McMaster Bujold](/wiki/Lois_McMaster_Bujold "Lois McMaster Bujold"), [C. J. Cherryh](/wiki/C._J._Cherryh "C. J. Cherryh"), [Cecilia Dart-Thornton](/wiki/Cecilia_Dart-Thornton "Cecilia Dart-Thornton"),[[27]](#cite_note-30) [Tanya Huff](/wiki/Tanya_Huff "Tanya Huff"),[[28]](#cite_note-31) [Mercedes Lackey](/wiki/Mercedes_Lackey "Mercedes Lackey"), [Charles de Lint](/wiki/Charles_de_Lint "Charles de Lint"), [Joan D. Vinge](/wiki/Joan_D._Vinge "Joan D. Vinge"), [David Weber](/wiki/David_Weber "David Weber"), [K. D. Wentworth](/wiki/K._D._Wentworth "K. D. Wentworth"), and [Catherine Asaro](/wiki/Catherine_Asaro "Catherine Asaro").

On February 20, 2005, the [Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America](/wiki/Science_Fiction_and_Fantasy_Writers_of_America "Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America"), which had honored Norton with its [Grand Master](/wiki/Grand_Master_Nebula "Grand Master Nebula") Award in 1984, announced the creation of the [Andre Norton Award](/wiki/Andre_Norton_Award "Andre Norton Award"), to be given each year for an outstanding work of fantasy or science fiction for the [young adult literature](/wiki/Young_adult_literature "Young adult literature") market, beginning with 2005 publications. While the Norton Award is not a [Nebula Award](/wiki/Nebula_Award "Nebula Award"), it is voted on by SFWA members on the Nebula ballot and shares some procedures with the Nebula Awards.[[29]](#cite_note-SFWA2012-32)[[30]](#cite_note-SFWAnorton-33)[[31]](#cite_note-SFAwards-nebula-34) Nominally for a young adult book, actually the eligible class is middle grade and young adult novels. This added a category for genre fiction to be recognized and supported for young readers.[[32]](#cite_note-35) Unlike Nebulas, there is a jury whose function is to expand the ballot beyond the six books with most nominations by members.

Norton received the [Inkpot Award](/wiki/Inkpot_Award "Inkpot Award") in 1989.[[33]](#cite_note-36)

## High Hallack Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Andre_Norton&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: High Hallack Library")]

The High Hallack Library was a facility that Norton was instrumental in organizing and opening. Designed as a research facility for genre writers, and scholars of "popular" literature (the genres of science fiction, fantasy, mystery, western, romance, gothic, and horror), it was located near Norton's home in Murfreesboro, Tennessee.[[34]](#cite_note-norton-37)

The facility, named after one of the continents in Norton's *Witch World* series, was home to more than 10,000 texts, videos, and various other media. Attached to the facility were three guest rooms, allowing authors and scholars the chance to stay on-site to facilitate their research goals.[[34]](#cite_note-norton-37)

The facility was opened on February 28, 1999, and operated until March 2004. Most of the collection was sold during the closing days of the facility. The declining health of Andre Norton was one of the leading causes of its closing.[[34]](#cite_note-norton-37)
